He seemed to hang on my every word, as though I were some Delphic oracle, and pressed me for my thoughts on the state of art in England. I confessed, with a laugh meant to lighten the moment, that while I see faults—bad taste among patrons and a lack of inspiration in some artists—I never meant to suggest there is a true decline. England, to my mind, remains a country of admirable men, its spirit shaped and strengthened by leaders of rare quality, from Mr Pitt to his successor, the Duke of Wellington. Their guidance gives me hope for our times. It is reassuring, too, to find in Hinze a kindred spirit, someone who shares my faith in England’s promise, even as we acknowledge its imperfections.

. Altogether, my father's England seemed to me lovable, laudable, full of good men, and having good rulers, from Mr Pitt on to the Duke of Wellington, until he was for emancipating the Catholics; and it was so far from prosaic to me that I looked into it for a more exciting romance than such as I could find in my own adventures, which consisted mainly in fancied crises calling for the resolute wielding of domestic swords and firearms against unapparent robbers, rioters, and invaders who, it seemed, in my father's prime had more chance of being real

. Then there are the Greeks who, by the help of Phoenician blood or otherwise, are objectionably strong in the city. Some judges think that the Scotch are more numerous and prosperous here in the South than is quite for the good of us Southerners; and the early inconvenience felt under the Stuarts of being quartered upon by a hungry, hard-working people with a distinctive accent and form of religion, and higher cheek-bones than English taste requires, has not yet been quite neutralised. So far shall we be carried if we go in search of devices to hinder people of other blood than our own from getting the advantage of dwelling among us

. They had virtues which incorporated themselves in wholesome usages to which we trace our own political blessings. Let us know and acknowledge our common relationship to them, and be thankful that over and above the affections and duties which spring from our manhood, we have the closer and more constantly guiding duties which belong to us as Englishmen." To this view of our nationality most persons who have feeling and understanding enough to be conscious of the connection between the patriotic affection and every other affection which lifts us above emigrating rats and free-loving baboons, will be disposed to say Amen. True, we are not indebted to those ancestors for our religion: we are rather proud of having got that illumination from elsewhere

. April 15, 1829

Today, my heart is heavy with thoughts of our Irish plight. As I walked through the streets of Dublin, I overheard whispers of discontent regarding our very existence in this land. It seems, in high English circles, there is a sentiment that we have been indulged too greatly. They believe that if we faced harsher measures, perhaps we would learn to be more obedient, less troublesome. It is disheartening to think that our suffering, our struggles for dignity and recognition as equals, are seen as signs of weakness rather than resilience.

I strive for modesty, recognizing that many believe we Irish should show humility in our aspirations. We are born to be servile in their eyes, forever etched in the lower ranks of society. Yet, I cannot help but feel the unyielding spirit of our ancestors calling us to rise above these unjust perceptions. They speak of a "strong Government" that should maintain order, but what they truly maintain is a divide—one that keeps us in shadows, unable to claim our rightful place among them.

We, the Catholics of this land, often wonder how much longer we must endure this silent oppression. I sometimes catch myself dreaming of a day when our faith and our identity are not seen as burdens but celebrated as part of the rich tapestry of this nation. Until that day comes, I must hold onto my modesty, a virtue that seems to be all they allow us. Perhaps through such quiet strength, we can one day demonstrate the nobility that they claim we lack. 

Yours in faith,
A Faithful Irish Catholic
